It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Politics of private regulation: ISEAL and the shaping of transnational sustainability governance Allison Loconto Institut National de la Recherche Agronomique (INRA) UR1326 Sciences en Société, Institute for Research Innovation and Society (IFRIS), Université Paris-Est Marne-la-Vallée, Champs sur Marne, France Eve Fouilleux* CNRS – CEPEL/Université de Montpellier 1, MOISA – CIRAD Centre de Coopération Internationale en Recherche Agronomique pour le Développement, Montpellier Cedex 5, France Abstract Scholars describe the proliferation of sustainability standards by multi-stakeholder initiatives as part of an organizational ﬁeld for sustainability. The aim of this article is to gain a better understanding of the institutionalization process of this global organizational ﬁeld by focusing on the case of the ISEAL Alliance (the global association for sustainability standards). We show how ISEAL puts speciﬁc strategies into place to both reinforce and expand the role and inﬂuence of sustainability standards. This institutional entre- preneurship consists primarily of two dimensions: institutionalizing macro-standards based on a market- driven and procedural vision of sustainability; and simultaneously legitimating both the tools and ISEAL through internal and external enrolments and entanglements. The characterization of ISEAL’s activities in this way brings politics back into the analysis of sustainability standard-related technical debates and extends our understanding of how the micro-dynamics within organizational ﬁelds are interdependent upon macro-dynamics outside organizational ﬁelds. Keywords: institutional entrepreneur, ISEAL, legitimation, sustainability, voluntary standards. 1. Introduction The proliferation of standards in agriculture, education, health, and ﬁnance illustrates the argument, ﬁrst put forward over ten years ago, that we live in a “World of Standards” (Brunsson & Jacobsson 2000). This proliferation is the result of attempts by different actors to construct standards that put forward particular visions of the world (e.g. Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Büthe 2010; Marx & Cuypers 2010; Tamm Hallstrom & Boström 2010; Büthe & Mattli 2011; Ponte et al. 2011). Standards are described as playing a fundamental role in institutional emergence and the creation of organizational ﬁelds (DiMaggio & Powell 1983) where standards shape, and are shaped by, diverse actors and institutional logics (Mendel 2001; Bartley 2007; Dingwerth & Pattberg 2009). These characterizations of standards suggest that actors use the development, Correspondence: Allison Loconto, INRA, UR1326 Sciences en Société, Institute for Research Innovation and Society (IFRIS), Université Paris-Est Marne-la-Vallée, 5, Bd Descartes, 77420 Champs sur Marne, France. Email: loconto@inra-ifris.org *Both authors have contributed equally to this paper. Accepted for publication 29 April 2013. bs_bs_banner Regulation & Governance (2014) 8, 166–185 doi:10.1111/rego.12028 © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd Page 2: implementation, and compliance with standards as ways to forward political agendas through seemingly technical activities (Bowker & Star 1999; Frankel & Højbjerg 2009). In this article, we focus on global standards dedicated to environmental and social sustainability, that is, sustainability standards. Studies of sustainability standards initially described single cases of standards, discussed their rules and procedures, or assessed their impacts in society. A growing body of literature now approaches sustainability standards within the broader frame of sustainability policies and politics (e.g. Dingwerth & Pattberg 2009; Bartley & Smith 2010; Bernstein 2011; Djama et al. 2011). Building on DiMaggio and Powell’s (1983) heuristic, Dingwerth and Pattberg (2009) analyze social and environmental standards initiatives and relate them to the development of a transnational organizational ﬁeld1 of sustainability. Although other governance instruments (e.g. international conventions and public policies) and actors also shape this ﬁeld, sustainability standards inﬂuence the form it takes and constitute micro-dynamic elements of the ﬁeld. These micro-dynamics are important for how we can understand the politics and processes that shape organizational ﬁelds, particularly in the midst of debate over the meaning of sustainability within and outside the ﬁeld. We seek to contribute to the literature by forwarding an empirical argument that illustrates how an institutional entrepreneur contributes to the shaping of an organizational ﬁeld by both institutionalizing and legitimating speciﬁed actors, tools, and practices. An organizational ﬁeld includes “two constitutive elements: a set of institutions, including practices, understandings and rules; and a network of organizations” (Lawrence & Phillips 2004, p. 692). We concentrate our attention on one particular organization, which is actually an organization of organizations, and its activities: the ISEAL Alliance. ISEAL is the global associa- tion for social and environmental standards and claims to be “work[ing] with established and emerging sustainability standards in sectors as diverse as agriculture, ﬁshing, carbon, water and more, to develop guidance and deliver programs that strengthen their social and environmental impacts” (ISEAL 2013b). ISEAL is a core actor in deﬁning the ﬁeld (Dingwerth & Pattberg 2009; Bernstein 2011), notably because of its proactive inﬂuence on mimetic isomorphic activities. Reinecke et al. (2012, p. 804) point out ISEAL’s coordinating role as “a market watchdog pro- tecting the legitimacy of the sustainability standards movement.” However, to date, an in-depth investigation of who this actor is, what it does, and how it builds legitimacy, is missing. This article attempts to ﬁll this gap by characterizing ISEAL’s institutional entrepreneurship as part of the institutionalization of the sustainability ﬁeld. The article proceeds as follows: First, we present our conceptual framework, which articu- lates the notions of institutional entrepreneurship and legitimation processes. Second, we present the characteristics of ISEAL as an organization. Third, we describe how ISEAL builds institutions through the production of meta-standards for the ﬁeld. Fourth, we analyze how ISEAL pursues internal and external legitimacy. Finally, we discuss how this institutional entre- preneurship contributes to the institutionalization of the ﬁeld and reﬂect on what this means in terms of sustainability. 2. Institutional entrepreneurship in contested ﬁelds With other authors, we see the work of standards development organizations (SDO) as shaping processes of institutionalization (Delmas 2002; Mattli & Büthe 2003; Bartley & Smith 2010). For example, while the tools of audit are a means to verify conformance and build trust in standards systems (Power 1997; Courville 2003; Prakash & Gugerty 2010), they also ensure that the standards are both implemented and legitimized (Mutersbaugh 2005; Gulbrandsen 2010; Politics of private regulation A. Loconto and E. Fouilleux © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 167 Page 3: McDermott 2012). These interconnected activities of standardization, accreditation, and certi- ﬁcation (Abbott & Snidal 2001; Loconto & Busch 2010) shape the organizational ﬁeld by deﬁning what one can audit, certify, accredit, and standardize. The creation of these boundaries is one of the ways to institutionalize an organizational ﬁeld. Yet actors debate the creation of these boundaries both within the emerging ﬁeld and in pre-existing ﬁelds. Indeed, “ﬁeld formation is not a static process; new forms of debate emerge in the wake of triggering events that cause a reconﬁguration of ﬁeld membership and/or interaction patterns” (Hoffman 1999, p. 351). Thus, controversy, uncertainty, or public debate often provide opportunities for institutional entrepreneurs (DiMaggio 1988)2 to mobilize their resources to push forward a preferred idea, technology, or practice (Hoffman 1999; Levy & Scully 2007; Levy et al. 2010). They engage ﬁeld structures and draw on pre-existing institutions in an attempt to change them or create new networks of relationships and institutions (Lawrence & Phillips 2004). Within the sustainability ﬁeld, we ﬁnd controversy in the debates over the proliferation of sustainability standards and the confusion this produces for consumers. The public campaigns to increase the market share of competing standards and the accusations of “greenwashing”place all sustainability standards under scrutiny and question their viability as instruments that can deliver sustainability outcomes (Arnold 2010). ISEAL seeks to quell some of this controversy within the ﬁeld to increase the credibility and expand the inﬂuence of sustainability standards. Dingwerth and Pattberg (2009) treat ISEAL as an example of the institutionalization of the organizational ﬁeld for sustainability; however, they claim, “the direct effects of the ISEAL Alliance are likely to be limited” (p. 726). We argue that ISEAL is not only an “example” of the emerging sustainability ﬁeld, but also a political actor in that ﬁeld. We refer to ISEAL as an institutional entrepreneur who consciously and actively participates in the institutionalization, consolidation, and spillover processes at stake. Our case reveals a dimension of this type of entrepreneurship that discussions of institutional entrepreneurs often gloss over; that is, political strategies materialize with the technical activities of setting standards and controlling their implementation (Garud et al. 2002; Frankel & Højbjerg 2009). Put differently, ISEAL’s entre- preneurship is more than purely setting the agenda, as many of its activities are rulemaking and seeking ways to enforce the rules (Keck & Sikkink 1998). We will show how ISEAL contributes directly and actively to constructing the three dimen- sions of a ﬁeld structure through its activities. Speciﬁcally, ISEAL demonstrates: “(i) an increase in interactions among organizations; (ii) an increase in the information load they share; and (ii) the development of mutual awareness of their involvement in the same debate” (Hoffman 1999, p. 352). 3. Legitimation processes Actors and the institutions they build must be recognized and accepted by the communities concerned, and, in particular, by the dominant actors in the pre-existing ﬁeld, in order to be considered legitimate (Cashore et al. 2004; Levy et al. 2010; Bernstein 2011). A number of scholars discuss legitimacy stakes in the context of standards systems and debate different possible requirements (Bernstein 2002; Boström 2006; Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Waddell & Khagram 2007; Beisheim & Dingwerth 2008; Black 2008; Keohane 2011). In terms of input and throughput legitimacy (Scharpf 1999; Fuchs et al. 2011), ISEAL describes its values as partici- pation, inclusion, transparency, accountability, information sharing, learning, and improve- ment. It also concentrates on credibility principles (sustainability, relevance, rigor, engagement, Politics of private regulation A. Loconto and E. Fouilleux © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 168 Page 4: impartiality, transparency, accessibility, truthfulness, and efﬁciency) (ISEAL 2013b), which serve to help actors distinguish between “credible” and “non-credible” standards. In terms of output legitimacy, ISEAL promotes “the need to balance effective performance with ease of uptake by enterprises and end users” (ISEAL 2013b, p. 3). Thus, there are elements of input, throughput, and output legitimacy in ISEAL’s goal for sustainability standard systems (and for itself as an organization). In order to promote this vision of legitimacy on the one hand, and the political project of expanding the use of voluntary standards on the other, ISEAL develops legitimation strategies. These are strategies developed by the actors “to demonstrate an aptitude to ensure the triumph of values” (Lagroye 1985, p. 400). We follow current approaches in the literature to study processes of “legitimation,” or ways of “seeking legitimacy” (Bernstein 2011). We contribute to these approaches through the use of the notions of enrolment and entanglement developed in actor-network theory (ANT) (Callon 1991; Rip 2010). When ANT is used in a study of controversy (Callon 1981), it serves as an analytical method ﬁrst for identifying new institutions, procedures, and concepts (Latour 2005); and second, for understanding how these are connected, how they are conﬁgured in technologies, and how interests are translated by actors (Callon & Law 1982). This helps the analyst to focus on both the problematization of issues and the locus of actions in order to reveal the politics of seemingly apolitical activities (Callon 1986; Latour 1987; Star 1991). By tracing actors and their instru- ments, we shed light on the processes through which they do the technical and political work of institutionalization. This allows for a deeper analysis of the role of standards as devices (Loconto 2010) in structuring organizational ﬁelds. In linking ANT to institutional entrepreneurship (Fouilleux 2004, 2009), we propose a dynamic way to take pre-existing institutions into account in the development of entrepreneur- ship strategies (Lawrence & Phillips 2004). For example, where enrolment refers to engaging actors in common activities and idea creation, the notion of entanglement means that all of the actors continue engaging each other so that the activities and ideas can persist through time. This furthers the analytic value of this approach by providing room for an institutional entrepreneur to evolve its strategies in response to what the other actors in the network are doing. However, an institutional entrepreneur is not always successful in entangling actors. Rather, we see actors constantly negotiating both the boundaries of the ﬁeld and their notions of legitimacy, while they are simultaneously constructing them. By adopting this dynamic and relational view of legitimation processes, we focus attention on actors, instruments, values, and the strategies employed to promote these values. This approach expands upon recent analysis because it explains the speciﬁc legitimacy concerns of actors. For example, we illustrate the political tensions that result from the high democratic legitimacy requirements and performance pressures observed in ISEAL (Bernstein 2011). This approach also extends our understanding of the relationship between the micro-dynamics within organizational ﬁelds and the macro-dynamics outside them (Dingwerth & Pattberg 2009) as these enrolments and entanglements transcend the ﬁeld. In the following sections, we apply this analytical approach to the case of the ISEAL Alliance based on empirical data we collected from 2008 to 2012. First, we conducted 15 semi-structured interviews with ISEAL staff, members, and ex-members, assuring the conﬁdentiality of interviewees. Second, we were participant observers in the ISEAL Assurance Code technical and steering committee, sponsored events (3), email lists, and standards-related international con- ferences (7). We did not use direct quotations from these observations, but rather quotations from interviews and publicly available documents (e.g. meeting minutes, public consultations) Politics of private regulation A. The ISEAL Alliance: An increasingly structured organization After a failed attempt two years before,4 ISEAL began in 1999 as a loose network of the leading social and environmental movements promoting standards at the time (often referred to as the “gold standards”): Fairtrade Labeling Organizations International (FLO), Forest Stewardship Council (FSC), International Federation of Organic Agriculture Movements (IFOAM), and the Marine Stewardship Council (MSC). In recognition of the overlap in their systems, the network began as “an Agreement in Principle which led to them meeting regularly to learn about each other’s programs and to ﬁnd ways to collaborate” (ISEAL 2013b). Initially, ISEAL was strictly an acronym for “International Social and Environmental Accreditation and Label- ing” and the Alliance was more of a platform for collaboration than a formal organization with strategic objectives. Ten years following their registration in the UK as a non-proﬁt company in 2002,5 ISEAL has become a bona ﬁde organization with political and market objectives, and an increased number of members. Discursively, ISEAL promotes a vision for the future: “a world where social justice and environmental sustainability are the normal conditions of business.” It expresses a ﬁrm belief in standards as the instrument: “ISEAL works from the premise that voluntary standards systems that are effective and accessible can bring about signiﬁcant posi- tive social, environmental and economic impacts” (ISEAL 2010b, p. 1). Finally, ISEAL deﬁnes a precise mission: “to deﬁne good practices for sustainability standards, to distinguish and promote credible standards, and to ensure that the people understand the difference” (ISEAL 2013c). This mission dictates rules for membership, dividing organizations into three categories (full members, associate members, and subscribers).6 Those international SDOs and accredi- tation organizations compliant with the ISEAL Codes of Good Practice are full members. These are the “top tier” of good social and environmental standards. ISEAL associate members (second tier) enter based on a pre-assessment and have one year from approval in which to meet full membership requirements. The third tier gathers a broader range of stakeholders, including emerging SDOs, which have committed to the ISEAL Code of Ethics. Although they declare their willingness to contribute to the ISEAL community, subscribers are not members and cannot receive ISEAL endorsement. In 2013, there were 13 full members, six associate members, and 46 subscribers (Table 1). These members are predominately SDOs and 87 percent are located in the Global North. The increase in the number of members accompanies a growth in staff: up to 17 people were working for the organization on a full-time basis in 2012. Most employees have high-level credentials (e.g. Masters and PhDs) and functions (“Managers” or “Directors”). Some employ- ees engage in organization-wide activities (e.g. monitoring and evaluation, communication, new codes). Others work directly with individual member organizations to help them comply with ISEAL rules in a “customer” style relationship.7 Member fees, ranging from €2,000 to €15,000 per year, fund some operation expenses, while the rest comes from donor funds.8 The ISEAL Board of Directors, elected from full members, is the governing body. As con- ﬁrmed by various interviewees, despite its recent opening to a rotation of all full members, the Politics of private regulation A. Loconto and E. Fouilleux © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 170 Page 6: founding members remain the most inﬂuential. The Secretariat develops work plans and sets the agenda for the organization. Consequentially it heavily inﬂuences ISEAL decisions, according to an ex-board member: Final responsibility is [supposed to be] the board.We should have a veto right. But we never got it! (. . .) Regarding standard-setting, all the Board can do is to approve the standard committee members’ appointment!9 Interviewees systematically mention the ﬁrst two executive directors as the “real” drivers of ISEAL. As such, they are responsible for ISEAL’s evolution from a loose network for exchange to a well-structured organization that favors expanding its membership. This is a key decision, which contributes to the idea that ISEAL must have a central role in “scaling-up” sustainability standards. ISEAL argues, “with 10 percent or more of global production certiﬁed in forestry, ﬁsheries and key agricultural commodities, sustainability standards have the potential to trans- form global markets. In order to achieve this, voluntary standards systems must scale up with unprecedented speed and efﬁciency” (ISEAL 2011b, p. 1). As the following sections describe, the decision by ISEAL to expand its membership has a number of important political consequences, particularly in terms of how they deﬁne sustainability. Although tensions and conﬂicts arise within internal technical debates, ISEAL never debates them publicly. These political dynamics are crucial for comprehending the insti- tutionalization of the ﬁeld. 5.
