The purpose of these tools is to provide other actors in society with a means to differentiate credible standards initiatives (i.e. ISEAL compliant members) from the “non-credible” ones. In addition to a general Code of Ethics, there are three main meta-codes: one for setting standards, one for measuring impacts, and one for overseeing assurance (certiﬁcation and accreditation rules). These codes consist of a series of speciﬁc requirements accompanied by illustrative, non-binding guidance that clariﬁes the intent. As noted above, ISEAL members must comply with these codes. In the following sections, we describe the content of these codes and the implicit deﬁnition they give to sustainability. 5.1. How to make a good sustainability standard? The Standard-setting Code ISEAL’s ﬁrst project was to develop a code of good practice for developing standards, in order“to improve consistency between standards, enhancing their effectiveness” (ISEAL 2010b, p. 3). Launched in 2004, this code described the procedures that an SDO should abide by when creating a sustainability standard. Various key aspects of the standard-setting process appeal to the multi-stakeholder nature of ISEAL: stakeholder mapping, balancing of interests, public consultation, and availability of standards. The existence of a stakeholder map at the beginning of the process allows the standard developers to balance diverse interests over time and ensure that they are consulting with interested stakeholders throughout the process. ISEAL insists that the absence of stakeholder participation would mean that such a standard is unnecessary or that the SDO did not seek participation properly. SDOs must reach consensus on the standard, and, thus, consensus becomes the end, rather than only the means, of standards setting. There must be at least two public consultation periods of 60 days each, where interested parties who are not necessarily involved in the standards development process can submit comments. SDOs review standards every ﬁve years, with the date publicly announced in the written standard. Finally, there is an additional requirement of publishing international standards in the public domain and providing translation where requested. This contributes to ISEAL’s claim that they provide public goods. In sum, the standard-setting code creates an outline for members’ standards, where the requirements focus on the structure of the documents and the procedures that must be in place. The focus is on the inclusion and participation of stakeholders. Apart from the speciﬁcity of the application of this code to sustainability standards, there is no mention of sustainability in the code. In other words, sustainability refers to a category of standards and not the content of the standard. ISEAL’s recent decision not to use content ﬁlters (i.e. the content of member’s standards) reinforces this point. In 2012, a debate ensued over the allowance of genetically modiﬁed organisms (GMOs) by prospective ISEAL members (e.g. the Round Table on Respon- sible Soy [RTRS]). Based on the argument that procedures and stakeholder processes “help ensure a good standard is created that will promote meaningful change (. . .) on the ground, the Board took the decision not to discriminate on this criterion.”10 In their view, taking a stance against GMOs would “fundamentally change the nature of ISEAL as a neutral organization representing a wide range of sustainability standards with diverse social and/or environmental objectives” (Mallet 2012). 5.2. Monitoring sustainability: The impacts code A core element of ISEAL’s strategy is to prove that its members’ standards have positive impacts in order to promote their use by consumers, governments, companies, and donors. ISEAL pursues this by developing a standardized methodology for impact assessment. The Politics of private regulation A. Loconto and E. Fouilleux © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 172 Page 8: impacts code (established 2010) sets out a structure for members to measure change. It includes requirements and guidance on which issues to measure and how best to measure them. It suggests principles that should guide impact assessment: appropriate scope, practical focus, quality, openness (transparency), effective communication, broad participation, learning and improving, and institutional capacity. The code separates impacts into the impact of what and the impact on what (ISEAL 2009b). For example, discussions at the 2011 annual meeting revolved around whether members should be measuring their impact based on achieving what is written in their standards or achieving their “missions” (i.e. fair trade, environmental conservation). Historically, members faced difﬁ- culties in proving their impacts because of ambiguity on what they were measuring. Often, they conﬂated the outcomes of speciﬁc standards interventions to an overall impact of the organization. In order to remedy this, the code introduces a “Theory of Change” approach to impact assessment that pushes members “to deﬁne and document the changes they intend to bring about and collect the data that will allow them and others to assess their contributions toward that change” (ISEAL 2011a, p. 2). This methodology encourages stakeholder partici- pation and organizational learning within and among ISEAL members (ISEAL 2009b, p. 3). The data collected are supposed to be turned into knowledge for improving, not only the impact of the standards, but also to contribute to external impact evaluation studies (ISEAL 2009a). ISEAL retains the power to assess member performance in terms of compliance to the code. Moreover, by relying on external evaluators ISEAL positions itself as a gatekeeper in the ﬁeld. Such an approach points to the broader vision of social change that ISEAL promotes: making sustainability the norm of business.Yet the problem remains that ISEAL does not delineate what sustainability is or ought to be. This controversy points to a practical and conceptual disconnect that appears in ISEAL’s work. ISEAL forwards a procedural approach in standards content and simultaneously urges members to evaluate their impacts on speciﬁc social and environmental changes. 5.3. Disciplining certiﬁcation by codifying assurance The ISEAL Assurance Code (launched in 2012) deﬁnes the types of certiﬁcation and accredi- tation that are necessary for the “credible” use of sustainability standards. As we underlined in the ﬁrst section, codifying certiﬁcation and accreditation is essential for ISEAL to deﬁne the boundaries of the ﬁeld. The code also highlights another important aspect of institutional entrepreneurship, which is that institutional entrepreneurs are constantly constrained by pre-existing ﬁelds, rules, and dominant actors in these ﬁelds. Particularly limiting is the EU Regulation (EC) 765/2008 (EC 2008). This regulation interprets accreditation to be the sole mandate of national accreditation bodies and the only standard for accreditation to be the International Organization for Standardization’s (ISO) version.11 Consequently, ISEAL needed to negotiate a politically and legally sensitive issue with their terminology. Speciﬁcally, the Assurance Code uses the words “oversight” rather than “accreditation,” and “assurance” rather than “certiﬁcation,” to avoid potential legal disputes. Another constraint is the relationship between ISEAL’s Code and ISO. The debate focuses on whether the Assurance Code should replace the ISO standards or merely be guidance documents that can help members in their obligatory compliance with the ISO standards. The ISO standards are the normative references for ISEAL member’s certiﬁcation and accreditation activities. ISEAL requires that members comply with ISO 17011 for accreditation bodies and ISO Guide 65, Politics of private regulation A. Loconto and E. Fouilleux © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 173 Page 9: 17021, or equivalent for certiﬁcation bodies linked to the SDO members (e.g. FLO-Cert). This reliance upon ISO guides and the third-party certiﬁcation model is a highly controversial issue among members who use different models and want ISEAL to sanction these models. ISO is also a point of tension between members and the Secretariat, which has been accused of forcing members to “isofy” their systems.12 As a result, the code committees investigated the relative advantages of different models of veriﬁcation (ﬁrst-, second-, third-party, and combinations). They created a non-hierarchical list of “models of assurance” and attempted to outline when these were appropriate. Some stake- holders expected a ranking of “most credible” models to occur. For example, one person argued, “ISEAL should be prepared to say that certain types (which ones?) of assurance system[s] can indeed provide a higher ‘level’ of assurance” (ISEAL 2012, p. 30). However, this did not happen. Rather, there was an important disagreement in the committees over whether third-party assur- ance and third-party oversight models (separation of standard setting, certiﬁcation, and accredi- tation into three independent organizations), which are enshrined in the ISO standards, are the “most credible” in all situations. “Pushed” by the ISEAL Secretariat,13 some members created their own international accredi- tation bodies to accredit third-party certiﬁers14 and promote the ISO model (e.g. Social Account- ability Accreditation Services [SAAS], Accreditation Services International [ASI]). Other members have structures where the users of the standards are also the creators of the standards, but rely upon third-party certiﬁcation and proxy accreditation for ISO 65 by a national accredi- tation body (e.g. 4C Association, Union for Ethical Biotrade [UEBT], FLO). Additional models focus on self-assessment with random third-party audits (e.g. RSB) and participatory guarantee systems (e.g. IFOAM) to ensure compliance. Moreover, some members see risks in the ability of a scheme owner to control the enforcement of its standards when they enroll “independent” actors in the network.15 This poses a challenge to the ideology that independence equals impar- tiality, which drives the ISO codes. As a consequence of these tensions and disagreements, numerous revisions shifted the code from a prescriptive document to one based on the principles of credible assurance: consistency, rigor, competence, impartiality, transparency, and accessibility.16 The requirements outlined in the standard set out the various forms and mechanisms of oversight that are needed to deliver these principles. The consensus was that: “the value of the Code lies in deﬁning the higher level principles for robust assurance and [that] we should not get too tied up in the details.”17 An example of this is in the requirement pertaining to conﬂicts of interest in the management of the assurance system. ISEAL’s deﬁnition of conﬂict of interest is not a mandatory requirement of the standard; instead, the procedure for dealing with conﬂicts of interest is. This approach is prevalent throughout the document, particularly on prescriptions where committee members did not achieve consensus (e.g. risk assessment at the level of the assurance system and sampling techniques). In sum, these three codes promote a vision of sustainability that is essentially procedural. What counts in the judgment of the quality of a standard is not the political project it defends, nor the content of the standard, nor its technical prerogatives. Rather, what counts is the number of stakeholders involved (type undeﬁned) or the number of days that the standard is open to public consultation on the Internet. Sustainability is not whether or not the standard has a social or environmental impact, but whether the procedures are in place to allow an evaluation or a learning process to occur. Finally, it does not matter how certiﬁers provide assurance of sustainability. Instead, members need extensive oversight procedures that ﬁt within ISEAL’s list of assurance models. Politics of private regulation A. Loconto and E. Fouilleux © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 174 Page 10: 6. Seeking internal and external legitimacy ISEAL’s main strategy as an institutional entrepreneur in the ﬁeld primarily consists of building meta-standards, or rather “standards for standards.” Yet in order to garner support for their project, ISEAL “seeks legitimacy” by enrolling and entangling speciﬁc internal and external actors. 6.1. Internal legitimacy: Enrolling members The ﬁrst dimension of ISEAL’s internal legitimacy lies in its identity as a civil-society organiza- tion: “we are a member-based organization. The rules are deﬁned by the members themselves. That means we can change the rules. It is not a top-down approach.”18 However, ISEAL also develops dynamic strategies to build its legitimacy in the eyes of its present and future members. To do so, ISEAL must cope with its diverse member base. ISEAL organizes many different types of SDOs and the content of their standards varies signiﬁcantly. First, this appears in the phi- losophy of the standards (e.g. the vision of sustainable agriculture diverges drastically between UTZ and organic). Second, the types of indicators used are diverse: progress indicators (e.g. FSC, 4C) versus pass/fail criteria (e.g. organic). Third, differences also exist in terms of membership conditions. Some standards set the bar for compliance very high in the belief that users will make changes to their systems before reaching compliance (e.g. organic, Rainforest Alliance [RA]). Others have a mid-level bar with internal progress requirements (e.g. FLO), while the rest are baseline standards where the focus is on including 100 percent of producers and then improving their practices within the scheme (e.g. 4C, RSB). In addition to the market competition between member standards, this variety of content poses potential conﬂicts among members, and, thus, internal threats for ISEAL’s integrity. Such threats grow as ISEAL’s membership expands. To cope with such constraints, ISEAL develops internal strategies to create cohesion. For example, ISEAL uses a Code of Ethics and a program to remove competition between its members. Both of these internal strategies require members to share information, seek oppor- tunities to collaborate, create efﬁciencies between systems, develop agreements on communica- tion strategies, and refrain from making false or misleading statements about other members (ISEAL 2010a). As a result, ISEAL has begun to serve a justiﬁcatory purpose to quell intra- member competition within the market. This is illustrated below in the response by one of the leading RA certiﬁed tea companies to criticism from the fair trade community: We (. . .) believe that it isn’t fair for ISEAL certiﬁcation schemes to campaign against each other while so much of the tea on the market isn’t certiﬁed at all. (PG Tips 2010) Another strategy deployed by ISEAL to maintain cohesiveness lies in the focus on procedures described in the previous section and its reluctance to deﬁne sustainability clearly and concretely. However, this positioning of ISEAL as a neutral convener has revealed some tensions, demon- strated by the recent changes in membership. Numerous newcomers are part of the “scaling up strategy;” but two of the founding organizations are no longer members: Social Accountability International (SAI) and IFOAM. There was no public announcement regarding their departures and non-ofﬁcial accounts provide different justiﬁcations. According to SAI, its departure was merely a reallocation of the responsibility of scheme ownership, and budget, following ISEAL’s insistence on the creation of a separate accreditation body.19 When SAI created SAAS as their specialized third-party accreditation body, SAAS became the scheme owner for the SA8000 standard and, thus, took over SAI’s ISEAL membership. Politics of private regulation A. Loconto and E. Fouilleux © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 175 Page 11: Consequentially, SAAS was particularly adamant about the need to rank credible assurance models in the assurance code, given the investments SAI and SAAS made in order to follow the ISO model. The case of IFOAM’s departure is quite different. ISEAL staff referred to it as a “non-event,” mentioning internal IFOAM budgetary concerns as the main cause. In fact, IFOAM staff con- tinue to participate in ISEAL events and vice versa. However, the picture appears more political when speaking with IFOAM. Its executive director publicly declared,“we had to leave ISEAL. We stepped out because we could not actually ally with those people – too much greenwashing!”20 According to one IFOAM Board member, the unwillingness of ISEAL to reach a common deﬁnition of sustainability and its desire not to distinguish between levels of sustainability among members is the main reason for IFOAM exit. He commented: We founded ISEAL as a common forum. (. . .) and it started to grow and its emphasis changed (. . .) Our concern was and still is that as ISEAL gets many members – agriculture members, sustainability is deﬁned as GAP [good agricultural practices] only. They were supposed to have a continuous improvement process but this did not take place. (. . .). At a certain point, there was a discussion in London on ISEAL as a meta-brand. We could not accept this! Then we understood that it was time to think about our relation with ISEAL.21 This quote underlines that the controversy revolves around the political project supported by ISEAL through its “no content ﬁlters” approach to sustainability. The importance of ISEAL as an organization remains. In the words of the same interviewee: ISO is countries and nations whereas ISEAL is civil society. This is still the great strength of ISEAL; and this is why we would join ISEAL again if they would address our concerns. We do not want to be presented as the same level of sustainability as GAP. Standards must move from GAP towards organics. We could interpret IFOAM’s departure as a threat to ISEAL’s legitimacy, particularly because IFOAM is a founding member and is widely recognized as the “gold standard” for ecological sustainability. However, some actors read this split in a way that shows IFOAM as an outlier within ISEAL.A renowned sustainability standard expert reacted to the news in this way:“Really? IFOAM has left ISEAL? Well done. This is good news. They will not make trouble anymore.”22 Indeed, we can read the split as the victory within ISEAL of one deﬁnition of sustainability (more inclusive in terms of SDOs and less stringent in terms of farming practices) over another one (less inclusive and more stringent). This victory feeds into how ISEAL seeks external legitimacy. 6.2. External legitimacy: Entangling different publics ISEAL seeks external legitimacy through a process of enrolling and entangling actors and institutions within and outside the ﬁeld. The creation of the Stakeholder Council in 2010 is a good illustration. This advisory body to the Board of Directors makes recommendations about ISEAL standards and provides strategic advice to the Board. Under the presidency of RA, the current Council members include civil society organizations (World Wildlife Fund [WWF] Pronatur in Peru), a private ﬁrm (Unilever), and a governmental organization (GIZ, the German cooperation agency). These individuals were chosen based on their expertise and are supposed to “act as ambassadors within their communities for awareness and uptake of the Codes and credible standards systems” (ISEAL 2013a). The Council is clearly a strategy to increase and diversify stakeholder involvement in the promotion of ISEAL’s vision. However, formal legiti- mation devices are insufﬁcient and ISEAL uses more subtle enrolments. Politics of private regulation A. Loconto and E. Fouilleux © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 176 Page 12: First, ISEAL must prove that it does not aim to destabilize existing institutions and the corresponding global order. This is particularly the case for the relationship between ISEAL and the two dominant institutions in global trade: ISO (for private conformity assessment require- ments) and the World Trade Organization (WTO) (for legally mandated trade barriers). In developing the standard-setting code, for example, ISEAL drew directly from ISO and WTO (ISEAL 2010b). It explicitly states that, where relevant, compliance with the WTO Technical Barriers to Trade Agreement should take priority. As already mentioned, ISEAL relies heavily on ISO standards to establish what it considers “credible” assurance systems. Furthermore, ISEAL is involved in the review process of ISO standards through the Secretariat’s membership in the International NGO Network on ISO. This illustrates the reluctance of ISEAL to disturb the existing ISO dominance, despite continuing controversy. Second, ISEAL enrolls and entangles the private sector, especially multinational corpora- tions, in its network. In addition to the Stakeholder Council, ISEAL created a Business Forum as a means to engage companies within the ISEAL community. The 2011 and 2012 ISEAL annual meetings headlined a number of representatives from enrolled companies (e.g. Mars and Kraft) as plenary speakers. These efforts accompany commitments from a range of ﬁrms not only to source certiﬁed sustainable products, but also to collaborate with ISEAL and its members on projects that support this vision (Koritzke 2012). Third, ISEAL seeks to enroll the research community. For example, the second-largest group of ISEAL subscribers consists of researchers and consultants. In 2010, an email listserv was set up called the“Impacts Research”list and in July 2012, there were more than 310 subscribers.23 While involving academics is certainly instrumental for ISEAL in its claim to an evidence-based approach, this also poses risks to consensus building. While often quiet, a few debates took place in the group. One of particular interest discussed the need for comparative research on standards impacts. Although the researchers were adamant that comparative research is a valid method that can provide insights into “best practices,” ISEAL was particularly negative about comparing impacts: What I like about this study was its focus on the perceptions of clients (farmers and farm workers) in the standards schemes. What I don’t like about it is its focus on comparing different standards systems. This kind of research is what [someone from RA] has pointed out as unhelpful for all of us. I don’t mind that the study focuses on a number of standards schemes, but it does bother me that so much effort is wasted on comparisons across these schemes (. . .) As standards organisations (and in particular ISEAL members) are putting effort into collaborating [. . .] the attempt by researchers to compare different standards programmes is not providing any beneﬁt to the clients of standards systems.24 This quote illustrates how internal and external legitimating strategies employed by ISEAL may be conﬂicting as some communities are more difﬁcult to entangle than are others. Finally, ISEAL works with public organizations, which are often the most active ﬁnancial supporters of SDOs via their development policies and aid agencies (Fouilleux 2012). On an organizational level, ISEAL hired two staff to deal speciﬁcally with policy issues and commis- sioned studies to provide evidence of how to better engage the public sector in sustainability standards (ISEAL 2008). ISEAL actively engages intergovernmental organizations like the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO), the International Trade Centre, and the United Nations Committee on Trade and Development. It is also a member of the advisory committee of the United Nations Forum on Sustainability Standards (established in 2013). Such a self-promotion strategy seems to be successful, both in terms of symbolic recognition by public Politics of private regulation A. Loconto and E. Fouilleux © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 177 Page 13: institutions (e.g. FAO, UK Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs [Defra], Euro- pean Commission) and in terms of public funds raised (e.g. GIZ, State Secretariat for Economic Affairs [SECO]) (ISEAL Alliance 2012). For example, the European Economic and Social Com- mittee noted the need for harmonization by referring to ISEAL’s work and suggesting that: Resources could be made available to the ISEAL Alliance to take forward the work to harmonise schemes (. . .). ISEAL provides a multi-stakeholder organisation where the common aspects of various labelling schemes can be consolidated and where differences can be independently evaluated and explained. (EESC 2010, p. 7, para. 5.4) Such attribution, and the high number of ISEAL subscribers, attest to the ability of ISEAL to entangle some legitimating actors in the ﬁeld. Furthermore, ISEAL receives recognition as the gold standard for sustainability standards even when its staff is not present,25 as illustrated by the remark of an advisor to a Brazilian coffee SDO during an international conference on sustainable procurement: How do I know if a certiﬁcation scheme is credible? (. . .) Once you belong to ISEAL, your credibility as a standard rises up (. . .) If you want to be considered as credible, you must belong to ISEAL.26 The above quote also illustrates a shift in discourse around the legitimacy of ISEAL in the sustainability ﬁeld. In the beginning, ISEAL relied upon the reputation of its “gold standard” members. Now, its members and emerging standards legitimate their place by associating them- selves to ISEAL. This illustrates the reciprocity of enrolments, as enrolled actors are also pursuing their own interests (e.g. legitimacy, inﬂuence on other standards’ content, expertise, informa- tion) by becoming part of ISEAL’s network. 7. Discussion: Institutionalization and legitimation of sustainability standards The main argument we put forward in this article is that as an institutional entrepreneur, ISEAL focuses its attention on the technical aspects of standards systems to institutionalize and legiti- mate them in the sustainability ﬁeld. Speciﬁcally, ISEAL actively builds institutions to shape, provide cohesiveness to, and discipline the sustainability ﬁeld through the development of a series of meta-codes which fulﬁll two main purposes: harmonizing procedures among ISEAL members, and establishing the borders between insiders (credible standard schemes) and out- siders (non-credible standards schemes). Through this approach ISEAL appeals to a “form of mobilization which germinates in a community of expertise that shrouds change efforts in discourse of technique and formal rationality” (Zald & Lounsbury 2010, p. 981). As discussed above, ISEAL codes not only set rules for standard-setting, but also for the connected activities of certiﬁcation and accreditation, which are fundamental to the institutionalization of the ﬁeld. The construction of these institutions also requires enrolment and entanglement. Through these activities, we identify the two main goals that ISEAL pursues: i) to appear as legitimate in representing the sustainability standards community and governing the ﬁeld; and ii) to prove the acceptability/efﬁciency/superiority of sustainability standards as a regulatory option. To achieve these goals ISEAL develops a twofold strategy. The ﬁrst dimension is internal: it consists of managing consensus among members of the organization.
